raw_data:
b'
An interview with Michael Morton about Shopify's enterprise business, Amazon's long-term dominance, and host of other e-commerce topics.



View in browser

An Interview with Michael Morton About E-Commerce Winners and Losers

Thursday, April 18, 2024

Listen to this Update in your podcast player

Good morning,

This week\xe2\x80\x99s Stratechery interview is with Michael Morton . Morton is a senior research analyst at MoffettNathanson with a focus on e-commerce and marketplaces. Morton has worked on both the buy-side and sale-side of equity analysis, and also worked on the YouTube strategy team prior to joining MoffettNathanson.

In this interview we discuss a few fascinating research notes Morton has written recently: he\xe2\x80\x99s very bullish on Shopify\xe2\x80\x99s enterprise offering, even as he thinks that brands will end up on Amazon in the long run. We get into why he thinks both things are true, along with covering a host of other e-commerce topics: how COVID changed the structure of e-commerce, Shopify Audiences, Amazon Buy With Prime and advertising businesses, and why Walmart is poised to be the other big winner in the U.S.

As a reminder, all Stratechery content, including interviews, is available as a podcast; click the link at the top of this email to add Stratechery to your podcast player.

On to the Interview:

An Interview with Michael Morton About E-Commerce Winners and Losers

This interview is lightly edited for clarity.

Topics:
Background | The Barbell Effect in E-commerce | Shopify\xe2\x80\x99s Enterprise Opportunity | More on Shopify | Buy With Prime | Amazon Advertising | Walmart

Background

Michael Morton, welcome to Stratechery.

Michael Morton: Thanks for having me, Ben.

So tell me more about yourself, before we get into all the stuff that you cover, but how did you come into the position of being obsessed about e-commerce? What was the life path?

MM: Oh, that\xe2\x80\x99s a great way to start the conversation. My path is somewhat opposite of how most people go about the investment industry. Most people start in investment banking or the sell-side with the goal of transitioning to what\xe2\x80\x99s referred to as the buy-side. It\xe2\x80\x99s the people putting the money to work, paying the sell-side for research, I started on the buy side outside of New York area.

I needed to move to New York pretty quickly for my wife\xe2\x80\x99s residency and I joined a firm that Michael Nathanson and Craig Moffett once worked at called Bernstein Research, I was there for six years covering greater TMT [technology, media, and telecom]. Really enjoyed it and then YouTube reached out, they were looking for someone from Wall Street who covered global Internet for their strategy team. They had hired someone from Bernstein years prior, the Internet Analyst named Carlos Kirjner, they love the way that Wall Street people are straight talkers. No consulting spin, willing to look you have a face and tell you whether they disagree with something or not.

I was enjoying my time at YouTube, but I really missed the markets. It\xe2\x80\x99s what makes me tick, has me excited to jump out of bed every day, and I have been a admirer of Craig Moffett and Michael Nathanson for my entire career. So when Michael Nathanson calls you and says, \xe2\x80\x9cHey, we hear you\xe2\x80\x99re at YouTube. We\xe2\x80\x99re looking for an Internet Analyst, would you be interested?\xe2\x80\x9d It\xe2\x80\x99s a no-brainer for someone who\xe2\x80\x99s been reading their work for a decade, similar to how long I\xe2\x80\x99ve been reading your work.

Speaking of YouTube, you mentioned the Analyst for the Global Internet while being at YouTube. I\xe2\x80\x99m curious, what did you learn about the Global Internet at YouTube that surprised you? The reason I asked this is I remember, I think I\xe2\x80\x99ve told this story before, but when I was at Microsoft, my second day there, I went in for some monthly business meeting about Hotmail. \xe2\x80\x9cHotmail, who uses Hotmail?\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94 turns out an astronomical number of people use Hotmail. It was one of those formative experiences, just the casual way that they went through country after country and these massive numbers. It was just a feeling of scale and largeness that it\xe2\x80\x99s still kind of hard to describe what it was like, and because you\xe2\x80\x99re there in charge of one product, you become inured to it. I\xe2\x80\x99m curious if you had a similar sensation being at YouTube?

MM: It\xe2\x80\x99s funny you use that example because that\xe2\x80\x99s exactly what I was going to point to as my key learning. It\xe2\x80\x99s interesting because on the outside, when you\xe2\x80\x99re in the investment world, I poke fun of myself and call us model monkeys because our views of the world live in Excel and life is a lot more complicated than that, and much messier than a simple financial model. It was probably the second or third week, I really was overwhelmed by the vastness of YouTube\xe2\x80\x99s platform. It is larger, more complicated with greater user depth and complexity in this global product than you can ever imagine from the outside. Just the sheer amount of content uploaded every day and the effort to keep it cataloged, keep it sorted, keep it safe. It\xe2\x80\x99s a Herculean task.

Yeah. It\xe2\x80\x99s interesting because on the outside I think you can fail to appreciate that. Then on the inside, like I said, you just become numb to it after a while. It\xe2\x80\x99s like just two totally different worlds that can barely communicate with each other.

MM: It\xe2\x80\x99s funny, because you talk to investors and they\xe2\x80\x99ll point to a company and say, \xe2\x80\x9cThey should just do this: X, Y, and Z\xe2\x80\x9d. I remember I was sitting in on a presentation where a team was tasked to go tag YouTube content so they could measure how much of it was a certain form factor, without going into too great of detail on it. This exercise took six months and it\xe2\x80\x99s just so much so content, that when you\xe2\x80\x99re on the outside saying, \xe2\x80\x9cOh, A goes over here and B goes there\xe2\x80\x9d, it\xe2\x80\x99s a lot harder.

When there\xe2\x80\x99s actually meaningful compute costs that you would never even think about when you\xe2\x80\x99re running a little Excel model. Yeah, it\xe2\x80\x99s a different world.

The Barbell Effect in E-commerce

So you are focused on the Internet generally, but e-commerce is what I most closely associate you with. There\xe2\x80\x99s a thesis that you guys have had that it\xe2\x80\x99s no longer a \xe2\x80\x9ceveryone wins market\xe2\x80\x9d. What does that mean and how is that different from the way you thought about the world previously?

MM: I have to give credit to the title to Lisa Ellis , who was our payments analyst before she went to Visa. She read it a day or two before and I tried to have some catchy title and she said, \xe2\x80\x9cSounds like you\xe2\x80\x99re just saying this is no longer an \xe2\x80\x98Everybody Wins Market'\xe2\x80\x9d, that was how it switched to the title.

The genesis of this view is the reality that pre-COVID, not to suggest it was easy to run an e-commerce platform, but there were the digitally-native platforms that lived their entire lifespan in an e-commerce world. Use a Chewy, for example, Wayfair, Amazon.

The brick-and-mortar players, I use the comparison to Michael Nathanson\xe2\x80\x99s world of linear media, it\xe2\x80\x99s very similar to what went on between Netflix and the legacy media players. Brick-and-mortar had a good business, your merchants and vendors fronted you your goods, consumers came and purchased them, handled the logistics for you, and it worked pretty well. Why would you run into this industry where you needed to have perfect price transparency? You can just Google a product and you can see how everybody\xe2\x80\x99s price lines up, then you have to deliver these products to people\xe2\x80\x99s doorsteps, which is really expensive, and then they return it and they ship it back.

Well, COVID forced brick-and-mortar to get really serious about their e-commerce strategies quickly. You saw them go from bumbling along the bottom with market share to almost overnight picking up material market share and improving their strategies.

It was a blessing for firms like Amazon and if you have either the largest inventory, or what I\xe2\x80\x99ll refer to as SKU offering in the world with the best logistics network, or if you have unique inventory, it can be really good for you. Etsy, they have some of their own headwinds right now that I\xe2\x80\x99m sure we\xe2\x80\x99ll get the chance to talk about. Shopify, which sells some unique beauty supplies and vitamins and things like that that aren\xe2\x80\x99t replicated around all the brick-and-mortar retailers. If you\xe2\x80\x99re selling stuff that is not differentiated, dog food, shelf stable things, furniture that\xe2\x80\x99s become widely commoditized, the post-COVID world is going to be much harder for you as an e-commerce operator.

So it\xe2\x80\x99s interesting because some of the stuff you are saying there, I was just writing about this yesterday that one of the reasons, beyond the fact I\xe2\x80\x99m in the media, that I like to write about the media is that the media predicts a lot of the trends that will hit other industries just because media is easy to put online, so you see online effects very quickly. It\xe2\x80\x99s funny, you talk about this \xe2\x80\x9cEverybody wins\xe2\x80\x9d, including just people who started online, I think of all the Internet-native publishers that came along. We\xe2\x80\x99re not talking about your old stodgy newspaper, \xe2\x80\x9cOh yeah, they had a hard time making it online\xe2\x80\x9d, no, there was a lot of companies that started online with Internet assumptions and everybody seemed to be winning for a while. Then I remember I wrote in 2015, Popping the Publishing Bubble just saying, \xe2\x80\x9cLook, it\xe2\x80\x99s not just the newspapers that are screwed, every single digital publisher is screwed\xe2\x80\x9d, and it\xe2\x80\x99s the exact same thing that you were talking about. You\xe2\x80\x99re going to either have a platform with everything, which is basically the aggregators, Google, Facebook, et cetera, or you\xe2\x80\x99re going to have to be super highly differentiated with a business model to match. It sounds like COVID basically shoved all of retail into that paradigm to the extent something was pulled forward, that\xe2\x80\x99s what was pulled forward.

MM: A generational pull forward for a lot of these businesses, and I think market participants have been digesting that for the last two years. It wasn\xe2\x80\x99t immediately obvious, because there was a point in time where if you annualized what was going on in 2020, 2021, the numbers were just enormous. Then everyone realized, \xe2\x80\x9cWait, there\xe2\x80\x99s a lot more participants for these jump balls now\xe2\x80\x9d, and things are going to be a lot harder.

What\xe2\x80\x99s interesting is because now the common narrative is the pull forward didn\xe2\x80\x99t happen. Everything\xe2\x80\x99s retreated. What I think you are driving at, which is really interesting to me, is that, yes, maybe in terms of consumer behavior there was a pullback and then e-commerce has returned to its trend line, or retreated to its trend line is a better way to put it, but the structural changes are done. We\xe2\x80\x99re in the truly Internet era in terms of the barbell effect.

MM: Right. Retailers went from the balancing act of physical and digital to focusing more on digital. Now, I do think the consumer wants an omnichannel solution, the whole world doesn\xe2\x80\x99t go digital. We recently spoke about the trend that you were just discussing as well where it hit publishers and media players, but we\xe2\x80\x99re asking the question, \xe2\x80\x9cIs the Internet good for a lot of these companies?\xe2\x80\x9d.

Yeah, I have a whole section on that, because I think it\xe2\x80\x99s interesting.

MM: It\xe2\x80\x99s just tough. It\xe2\x80\x99s changed how a lot of these businesses have to operate and you have to be able to compete in the digital world and also offer omnichannel solutions and the consumer gets increasingly demanding.

Brands\xe2\x80\x99 E-commerce Choice

Let\xe2\x80\x99s jump into that, the apparel point. You wrote recently about branded apparel, the choices they need to make with e-commerce, balancing retail versus e-commerce, and what do they do for e-commerce? Like first-party Amazon, third-party Amazon, their own site via Shopify or whatever else. I assume though you were writing about branded apparel as a stand-in for basically everything, right? That wasn\xe2\x80\x99t a unique choice facing one particular category?

MM: We picked branded apparel because we thought it\xe2\x80\x99s a great canary in the coal mine to what is going on in certain industries that are really competitive, that can become commoditized quickly, consumer electronics and stuff like that. The trends can play out rapidly and you can get value destruction really quickly.

Branded apparel is interesting because you have these businesses that have spent decades building a brand, whether it\xe2\x80\x99s a luxury brand or an athletic brand, and they had good businesses. They sold a portion of their inventory through wholesale and then through their direct retail business. Once you cleared your monthly rent for your own retail stores, everything was gravy on top of that. Now, we\xe2\x80\x99re watching the behavior of companies that have tried to pull back from their wholesale distribution channels, and then not too long after, they come running back to them, they realize it\xe2\x80\x99s a lot harder to move this inventory in our own retail in a digital world.

Ben, you\xe2\x80\x99ve done a lot talking about Apple\xe2\x80\x99s ATT changes , but traffic acquisition is the tax that they all have to pay in the digital world. What we wanted to try to figure out in this report was where does it end up long-term? What has the best profit profile for them? Where we land is if you can get it, if you\xe2\x80\x99re big enough and if you bring enough to the table, an Amazon first-party relationship where they buy the inventory from you, they price it, they sell it, they deliver, is a really great comparison to the legacy business that you have. The problem is no brand wants to hand over their keys to Amazon, they lose pricing control.

I\xe2\x80\x99ve heard plenty of horror stories about being a first-party customer of Amazon. You get squeezed, if any other retailer drops your price, Amazon demands that you pay for the price to be dropped on their side as well, for example. Is your argument, it might be a reasonable one, \xe2\x80\x9cIs that still worth it?\xe2\x80\x9d

MM: I wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t want to be an executive running any of these branded apparel companies, I think they have a very tough dilemma on their hand. They look at Amazon\xe2\x80\x99s 1P [first-party] business. In year one or two, that\xe2\x80\x99s a better profit profile. If you look at their third-party business, you\xe2\x80\x99re handing over referral fees, you\xe2\x80\x99re paying for the fulfillment, you\xe2\x80\x99re trying to operate day in, day out, moving this merchandise. That\xe2\x80\x99s not as wonderful as you would like it to be. The independent freestanding e-commerce website, if you look at the digitally-native companies, the DTC companies that we\xe2\x80\x99re all familiar with, Warby Parker, Allbirds, you name it, they\xe2\x80\x99re advertising intensity runs 2x what these branded apparel companies who operate in both the physical and the digital world. If you want to call them the leading indicator to what these trends look like, it\xe2\x80\x99s really scary. You have these businesses, these branded apparel companies that, I don\xe2\x80\x99t want to get too deep in numbers because you\xe2\x80\x99ll lose an audience, but call it low teens operating income margins. If all of a sudden your ad intensity doubles, it\xe2\x80\x99s pretty destructive to your profit profile.

Don\xe2\x80\x99t worry. I don\xe2\x80\x99t think you can out-nerd our audience, so it\xe2\x80\x99s okay. This point is a good one. I remember I wrote an Article about Facebook and I think Harry\xe2\x80\x99s , or one of the razor blade companies, I can\xe2\x80\x99t remember which one it was, but basically pointing out that everyone looked at e-commerce as, \xe2\x80\x9cWow, look, you could keep all the margin\xe2\x80\x9d. You don\xe2\x80\x99t have to pay retailers, you don\xe2\x80\x99t have to do all these sorts of things, and you still have to acquire customers. I thought you had a really good bit in this note that rent was a fixed cost and people would walk up to your store, but what you have to pay on Facebook or Google to acquire that customer, that\xe2\x80\x99s variable. Oh, by the way, thanks to ATT and other things, they can extract ever more of your margin and you don\xe2\x80\x99t really have any other way to acquire customers, so you just have to eat it.

MM: The analogy I tried to use, there\xe2\x80\x99s a J.Crew in the town where I live, and it\xe2\x80\x99s on the main street. In that world, they pay their rent and it\xe2\x80\x99s a monthly fee, and what they sell on top of that is great. In the Internet world, you have to pay Facebook and Google every time someone walks down the street and in the door and it doesn\xe2\x80\x99t matter that you had to pay them for the person before that. Every time someone\xe2\x80\x99s walking in the door, you\xe2\x80\x99re paying Meta and Google no matter what, and that\xe2\x80\x99s a tough dynamic.

So basically the long and short of it is, all the horror stories that I was just articulating that you hear about Amazon and them squeezing you and pushing on you, at the end of the day, it may be better that knowing exactly who\xe2\x80\x99s doing it to you is better than being at the mercy of the Meta ad algorithm or whatever it might be?

MM: Yes, in the near term, but I would be scared. This is why I don\xe2\x80\x99t want to be an executive at any these companies, because day one, when you talk to people in the industry, Amazon actually pays a higher rate for wholesale units than a legacy kind of Macy\xe2\x80\x99s of the world. Where I would be terrified is year three or five when Amazon becomes a much bigger part of your business. Amazon looks at you and says, \xe2\x80\x9cYeah, guess what? Terms are changing.\xe2\x80\x9d

(laughing) Pray they don\xe2\x80\x99t change further.

MM: And I know on very good account that they do that in other aspects of their business.

How does the third-party opportunity on Amazon fit on there?

MM: Third-party? So it\xe2\x80\x99s tough to generalize these categories. We look at it through a framework of branded apparel, but you have so many different categories within Amazon and different profit profiles. It\xe2\x80\x99s still a good business, it\xe2\x80\x99s just very challenging because you lock in handing \xe2\x80\x94 if you\xe2\x80\x99re using the apparel industry, it\xe2\x80\x99s a 17% referral fee, which is consumer Internet terminology for take rate because people like to avoid that word.

And then you have to do advertising on top of it anyway.

MM: And then, yes, so for every unit you sell, you\xe2\x80\x99re handing over basically mid-twenties percent of every dollar that goes in and then you\xe2\x80\x99re paying fulfillment fees, which run somewhere in the teens, so it\xe2\x80\x99s costly as well. But the incredibly valuable thing Amazon brings to the table is consumer traffic, right? It solves this enormous headache that entrepreneurs and Internet merchants face every day and you hear this comparison every now and then where someone will talk about, \xe2\x80\x9cOh, Shopify\xe2\x80\x99s attach rate code for take rate is called 3%, what a discount versus Amazon\xe2\x80\x9d. My first remark is, \xe2\x80\x9cNo, no, no. The Shopify merchants have to wake up and go find customers everyday and they quickly end up being above the 20% take rate of Amazon.\xe2\x80\x9d

So what does it take to succeed in this market? Because the obvious response would be under these pressures, if you\xe2\x80\x99re being driven to be a commodity, you have to really drive down your cost structure or you have to be highly differentiated, sort of the barbell effects we were talking about. But the challenge is you had this great line, you said in a note about this, \xe2\x80\x9cE-commerce continues to demonstrate an ability to take goods that were once branded with pricing power and reduce them to near commodities of consumers making purchasing decisions based off of 5-star review system from their peers\xe2\x80\x9d. Can you expand on that? That strikes me as a pretty profound statement.

MM: Yeah, I think it\xe2\x80\x99s terrifying for people who\xe2\x80\x99ve built brands because marketplaces \xe2\x80\x94 I\xe2\x80\x99m biased as a marketplace analyst, I think they\xe2\x80\x99re some of the best business models we\xe2\x80\x99ve seen, but they have this incredibly commoditizing impact as goods sit right next to each other.

You can talk about, I\xe2\x80\x99m picking examples, but a rain jacket, a black raincoat, you have a North Face sitting there, a Columbia, and then a couple other brands on the marketplace and they look the same. The next thing you see below there is price and yet you see the reviews and all of a sudden the thing that\xe2\x80\x99s 20% cheaper has an equivalent star review. It\xe2\x80\x99s really hard to get around that force, it\xe2\x80\x99s almost as reliable as gravity, so I don\xe2\x80\x99t know the answer.

What we try to get across in that note is unless you\xe2\x80\x99re a digitally-native and founded company and you\xe2\x80\x99re not cannibalizing a legacy business, so that\xe2\x80\x99s Amazon, Google, Netflix, Meta of the world, the Internet is a very challenging transition for you. There are certain businesses that have incredible consumer loyalty, but what we tried to do in that report also is we looked at direct traffic, organic traffic, organic Google search, or direct to the website and for almost everyone, it\xe2\x80\x99s down and to the right. It just plays back into that problem of having to pay the traffic acquisition cost again and again.

What you can find the occasional brand that has pretty stable direct traffic, one that surprised me, I\xe2\x80\x99m not an expert in female apparel, but Victoria\xe2\x80\x99s Secret was pretty stable web traffic, organic web traffic and I guess that makes sense because there\xe2\x80\x99s not a lot of switching, right?

Yeah.

MM: A branded t-shirt though, or a new pair of sneakers, not a lot of people buy the same sneaker for thirty years straight.

I do! Kizik.com. There\xe2\x80\x99s your DTC recommendation. The only shoes that I wear, I was not paid to do this, they should pay me given how many pairs of shoes I bought over the years.

A couple of things that are building on this, this is very thought-provoking for me. So number one, not to step on Michael Nathanson\xe2\x80\x99s turf, but there is sort of a Netflix comp here in that I think he \xe2\x80\x94 or I don\xe2\x80\x99t know, I won\xe2\x80\x99t speak for him \xe2\x80\x94 there is a sense that a lot of these companies, they saw the sort of like, \xe2\x80\x9cOh, we could go direct to customers. Why? We have the content. That\xe2\x80\x99s what matters.\xe2\x80\x9d It turns out it\xe2\x80\x99s a really hard job. Customer acquisition costs are killing them, churn is killing them even more, and there\xe2\x80\x99s a bit where if you are a legacy company, you look at the idea of being aggregated, of being subject to this entity that actually is between you and the customer. Often the best business choice is just to do it, you just have to take it, that\xe2\x80\x99s just the reality. You\xe2\x80\x99re fighting against structural forces that are bigger than you can control, and your responsibility to your shareholders and to what you do is to sort of accept reality.

There\xe2\x80\x99s a bit where I\xe2\x80\x99m hearing a similar theme from you. I actually find it pretty shocking, your bit, but you should probably just sell to Amazon and, yeah, they might screw you in three to five years, but you might not have much choice in the matter. But then I think about other industries and, yeah, that\xe2\x80\x99s sometimes just what you got to do.

MM: Depends on your perspective, but that is if it\xe2\x80\x99s good or bad, but that\xe2\x80\x99s the conclusion that we reached and it\xe2\x80\x99s the big aggregators, as you\xe2\x80\x99ve coined the term very well. Amazon gets almost 500 million unique monthly visitors on their website and hundreds of millions of app downloads and monthly active users. We estimate 95 million U.S. Prime members if you think about eligible households below the poverty line. It\xe2\x80\x99s an incredibly challenging force to try to fight against, you\xe2\x80\x99re standing against the tide, right?

Yeah and your brand such that it matters is a brand that people will search for on Amazon. The way you think about branding has to change , it\xe2\x80\x99s not a brand that people will recognize in the grocery store on an end cap or whatever it might be. It\xe2\x80\x99s, \xe2\x80\x9cWill they know to type it in a search box?\xe2\x80\x9d, and that\xe2\x80\x99s a different challenge.

MM: That\xe2\x80\x99s the debate that I\xe2\x80\x99ve heard articulated from a lot of different apparel brand leads where the critique of a marketplace is actually not the marketplace\xe2\x80\x99s fault, it\xe2\x80\x99s your fault and you do have the potential to do clever merchandising within a marketplace that I find hard to swallow because when you look at the consumer experience on Amazon, I refer to it as seek-and-destroy. I don\xe2\x80\x99t do much browsing, I don\xe2\x80\x99t know many people that do much just perusing through Amazon pages to be inspired, right? \xe2\x80\x9cI need this, and this is where I know I can find it\xe2\x80\x9d, and it\xe2\x80\x99s in the feed and the bigger brands pay for the sponsored ads and they end up to the top of the feed, so it\xe2\x80\x99s a challenge.

Well, the other point that I am thinking about as you\xe2\x80\x99re talking about this is, in publishing, the response from my perspective is to go micro. To be super small, you keep your cost structure under control, and then differentiate, you find a limited base of customers, charge them a lot, and you can have a very nice business.

What I think is so challenging about e-commerce is it\xe2\x80\x99s the inverse of, \xe2\x80\x9cLook, all this disruption hit publishing first because publishing is super easy\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94 it\xe2\x80\x99s literally text, right? The Internet is made of text. When it comes to goods though, you have to make them and you have cost of goods sold, and you have inventory costs, and you have to worry about making too much or too little, all of which benefits from scale. So on one hand that protected these industries from the Internet for a long time, but now that they\xe2\x80\x99re dealing with Internet effects, it\xe2\x80\x99s actually much more challenging even than something like the publishing or media space just because cost of goods sold is a really hard thing to manage.

MM: You hit the nail on the head in this industry. Scale is a tremendously powerful force and it\xe2\x80\x99s a feedback loop that just continues to power the machine with the greatest scale and it lowers their unit costs from their cost of goods perspective, but then also creates a better consumer experience, which then aggregates the more consumers and you just \xe2\x80\x94 it steps up and steps up and steps up.

Shopify\xe2\x80\x99s Enterprise Opportunity

Let\xe2\x80\x99s go in the other direction. So if you\xe2\x80\x99re doing your own site, and this is very much in the other direction, actually, which I want to ask you about. You\xe2\x80\x99ve been writing a lot about Shopify and you wrote at the beginning of a recent Shopify note where you upgraded the stock to outperform, and this is another great quote: \xe2\x80\x9cLet\xe2\x80\x99s start by addressing the elephant in the room. Shopify trades at an elevated multiple. Upgrading to outperform from market perform implies expectations for an inflection in the business that consensus is currently missing.\xe2\x80\x9d That\xe2\x80\x99s a bold statement and a strong call. What\xe2\x80\x99s your sort of view on Shopify? What is this inflection point that you think the market is missing?

MM: Yes, and it\xe2\x80\x99s funny to follow the conversation that we just had about how Amazon is, the inevitability of Amazon.

Right, that\xe2\x80\x99s one of the things I want to ask you about. Exactly.

MM: To be blunt, it\xe2\x80\x99s a dueling battle that goes on in my head and I think in the marketplace as well. I know a lot of people that own Amazon and own Shopify and it is further down the road and that\xe2\x80\x99s what inspired us to write that report about, \xe2\x80\x9cCan they both win and what\xe2\x80\x99s going on at branded apparel? Can they both succeed?\xe2\x80\x9d

In the near term, what got us really excited about Shopify \xe2\x80\x94 we weren\xe2\x80\x99t when we first started our coverage because we weren\xe2\x80\x99t real believers in the fulfillment business that\xe2\x80\x99s incredibly capital intensive \xe2\x80\x94 but when they divested that , and we started refreshing our view on what Shopify\xe2\x80\x99s product offering was, it became pretty quickly apparent to us that while we were all distracted by the fulfillment business \xe2\x80\x94 no one could see past it, right? You took a software/payments business, and all of a sudden you\xe2\x80\x99re going to try to deliver things to people\xe2\x80\x99s door, and it was just blinders \xe2\x80\x94 what we realized was they, in the last year, had put together an enterprise-capable e-commerce offering.

The reason why that is so important is because the historical bread and butter of Shopify, as they\xe2\x80\x99ll say, is to arm the rebels. \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99re going to going to help everyone fight back against Amazon\xe2\x80\x9d. That\xe2\x80\x99s these merchants, 5, 10, 15 million dollars businesses, people fulfilling packages in their basement and taking them to the post office, and that\xe2\x80\x99s a wonderful product that [Shopify CEO] Tobi [L\xc3\xbctke] and his team have built. They scaled to $230 billion in GMV, but now half of that business has become the Shopify Plus product, which is for larger merchants.

Until we started realizing what was going on with the enterprise offering, it wasn\xe2\x80\x99t satisfying enough to necessarily justify where the stock was trading, and I operate in a world of, \xe2\x80\x9cWhat does everybody else think? And then what do you think? And then what\xe2\x80\x99s the multiple?\xe2\x80\x9d Expectation investing. What is a game changer is that Shopify removed the bottlenecks, the things that prevented them from being a solution to all the branded apparel companies that we were just referring to.

Historically, they did not have a good B2B solution. It was mostly a bit like B2C, and if you wanted a good B2B solution, you had to create your own separate page and login. It was just a headache for a lot of merchants to operate it. They didn\xe2\x80\x99t have a great international platform to roll you out in foreign markets, and they forced merchants to basically take everything at once. You couldn\xe2\x80\x99t a la carte the solution. The Nikes of the world, they\xe2\x80\x99re not going to just come and take everything you have to offer. They also had a variant limit, which sounds not material if you\xe2\x80\x99re like, \xe2\x80\x9cOh, you can have a hundred versions of a product,\xe2\x80\x9d but when you talk to anybody who operates in retail, if you just want to think about men\xe2\x80\x99s pants from size 28 waist to however far north they go in a couple of different colors, you are quickly through the 100-product variant limit.

They have removed all of these bottlenecks. They\xe2\x80\x99ve brought in new executives with enterprise software sales experience, they put Bret Taylor on the board who was the head of Salesforce for a period, and they\xe2\x80\x99ve gone from this what I\xe2\x80\x99ll refer to as SMB business or SMB-focused software company, to now adequately addressing the largest e-commerce players in the world. This is a shift that\xe2\x80\x99s occurred really in the last kind of 12-to-24 months and what we also saw was the industry that\xe2\x80\x99s involved in selling these solutions did a total 180. So they\xe2\x80\x99re referred to as system integrators, but it\xe2\x80\x99s your big consulting companies, right?

Deloitte, Accenture, etc. Yeah.

MM: You can look at when they made all of these product changes, and then almost overnight, they make these four big product changes and you see Accenture went out and acquired a Shopify agency. This is after they\xe2\x80\x99ve been just buying Salesforce agencies and Adobe-type agencies. They buy a Shopify agency, a couple other consulting firms buy Shopify agencies, they all announce partnerships.

So for us, we don\xe2\x80\x99t think the world is going to go all to Amazon overnight. You have a lot of big, strong enterprise brands who have struggled to keep pace with the innovation that occurs in e-commerce. They\xe2\x80\x99re looking around, wondering why they employ hundreds of developers and engineers and it\xe2\x80\x99s just hard to manage the innovation and the talent needs. Why try to struggle to do this on this home-built tech stack that you did twenty years ago when you have Tobi and [Shopify President] Harley [Finkelstein] and their team waking up every day, singularly focused on this, and now they have a solution that can solve your problems for you? That was the game changer for us.

What is Shopify bringing to the table such that if you remove the bottlenecks, they would be the obvious choice? Because there are other e-commerce platforms in the market and you mentioned Adobe or Salesforce or WooCommerce or Magento \xe2\x80\x94 are they still around? I don\xe2\x80\x99t know.

MM: Yes, yeah. Magento sits inside of Adobe.

What is it about Shopify that, just removing the bottleneck, would unlock this growth?

MM: What is so interesting about Shopify without doing an hour long debrief on all the different product offerings, they cover everything and I think it\xe2\x80\x99s pretty amazing when you look at it. If you or I woke up tomorrow and we\xe2\x80\x99re like, \xe2\x80\x9cLet\xe2\x80\x99s start a business together, Ben, selling XYZ\xe2\x80\x9d, they can help you make the logo to, they can help you come up with a company name, they\xe2\x80\x99ll lend you money, that\xe2\x80\x99s not really what the enterprise need. The enterprise can look at Shopify and see all the data that they\xe2\x80\x99ve put out and the Shopify checkout converts at a much higher rate than anything else.

Yeah.

MM: It\xe2\x80\x99s really intuitive.

It\xe2\x80\x99s a huge thing, yeah.

MM: They show this, and when you\xe2\x80\x99re dealing with e-commerce and these low single digit conversion rates, a 30% bump to a conversion rate can make the difference between thriving or dying and Shopify has a list of products like that that make these websites better, and they\xe2\x80\x99re constantly improving them.

What\xe2\x80\x99s really interesting was twice a year Shopify does these Editions and it\xe2\x80\x99s a big deal, they get all the people together, they release a hundred new innovations twice a year. If you look at their competitors, there\xe2\x80\x99s nothing even remotely close, and this is a space that\xe2\x80\x99s changing rapidly and they\xe2\x80\x99re running away from the competition.

We were at, I think it\xe2\x80\x99s the biggest e-commerce conference in the country, it\xe2\x80\x99s called Shop Talk in Vegas a couple of weeks ago, we were walking around chatting with people, and it became quickly apparent to us that there were a lot of Shopify employees who used to work at Salesforce. We went back to our hotel room and looked on LinkedIn and you search, I want to see people who were previous employer were Salesforce and are now at Shopify under the enterprise umbrella, and we found forty. We wrote it up in a report saying it looked like not only is the product better, but clearly, Salesforce sales team is getting a little frustrated with the commerce solution there, and they\xe2\x80\x99re joining Shopify. You could see also the start dates too. It looks like a concerted effort to go get some of these people.

Basically, Shopify is a good company. They get stuff done, they\xe2\x80\x99re innovative, but because they grew up just focused on the small and medium-sized business, all they needed to do was take what was good about the company and make it accessible. That explains it, it\xe2\x80\x99s like just remove the bottlenecks and suddenly it\xe2\x80\x99s much more-

MM: Make it so they can take one at a time. You don\xe2\x80\x99t want to give the whole keys over to Shopify if you\xe2\x80\x99re one of these big enterprise types.

You could take just Shop Pay , which is a big deal. What did you think about that? It used to be Shop Pay, you had to be a Shopify website. Now you can be another website and just use Shop Pay. It\xe2\x80\x99s very compelling if you\xe2\x80\x99re another website. To your point, it\xe2\x80\x99s amazing, it\xe2\x80\x99s the best buy button on the Internet by far.

MM: It\xe2\x80\x99s a game changer. I guess you could use the Trojan horse analogy. It\xe2\x80\x99s going to get their foot into the door, and it\xe2\x80\x99s going to just change that conversation going forward with the CTO or COO, they\xe2\x80\x99re going to be looking at Shop Pay, they\xe2\x80\x99re going to be looking at maybe one or two of the other commerce components solutions they\xe2\x80\x99re taking from Shopify, and they\xe2\x80\x99re going to ask their team, \xe2\x80\x9cWhy are we trying to do all these other things by ourself? Look how good the stuff is that we\xe2\x80\x99re using from Shopify,\xe2\x80\x9d so it\xe2\x80\x99s a great idea.

Now they\xe2\x80\x99re playing the long game because if you\xe2\x80\x99re just solely trying to manage to a take rate when you\xe2\x80\x99re selling to some of the larger enterprises and they\xe2\x80\x99re taking just maybe one or two of your products, it can be a smaller take rate in the near term. But if you\xe2\x80\x99re thinking long term, you\xe2\x80\x99re playing for a much bigger GMV pie when you do that, so we love that.

More on Shopify

So a couple of follow-on questions to that. You mentioned in your factors, you went through them, but one of them, you mentioned the big ISVs like Accenture and Deloitte. How important is it to understand the ISVs to understand enterprise software?

MM: We don\xe2\x80\x99t cover on our team the Accentures of the world.

No, no, but I was curious about them as a signal. I think Accenture said on the last earnings call or something that they had $450 million in bookings for doing generative AI stuff. There was a lot of jokes on the Internet like, \xe2\x80\x9cWow, at least someone\xe2\x80\x99s making money from AI.\xe2\x80\x9d But I\xe2\x80\x99ve heard from people on the ground that they\xe2\x80\x99re going in and doing basic RAG implementations with Llama 2. I don\xe2\x80\x99t know, it just strikes me that from an analyst perspective, it seems like at least from my perspective, and maybe I need to develop more contacts in this space to know what\xe2\x80\x99s going on, they\xe2\x80\x99re actually way more important than you think. There\xe2\x80\x99s a reason they always come up in enterprise keynotes because they actually are the ones that get stuff implemented.

MM: Yes. By saying I don\xe2\x80\x99t cover them the gut reaction from everyone in the sell-side when you\xe2\x80\x99re about to pontificate on something, that\xe2\x80\x99s not under your cover.

(laughing) Oh, got it sorry, I didn\xe2\x80\x99t mean to interrupt you. I apologize.

MM: No, no, I was like, I don\xe2\x80\x99t cover them, but, and then I\xe2\x80\x99m going to have some ideas. It\xe2\x80\x99s essential, I think, when you\xe2\x80\x99re dealing with the enterprise level to work with these firms due to the specialty in which they are focused. If you are a large enterprise e-commerce company, you\xe2\x80\x99re not going to try to do some rip-and-replace on your own for the first time.

Right.

MM: There\xe2\x80\x99s enormous career risk. What you want to do is have someone from an Accenture come in who for a living takes home-built tech stacks or Adobe or Salesforce and rip-and-replace. That\xe2\x80\x99s all they do every day of their life, they\xe2\x80\x99ve seen every error that can go on, all the problems that pop up, and I think you\xe2\x80\x99re happy as the executive of one of those teams to help pay and outsource some of the career risk involved in a transition like that. But we have had conversations with, and Shopify actually had a mattress company at their booth giving a talk about they did it themselves, they had their own home-built tech stack, but they\xe2\x80\x99re much smaller, right?

Right.

MM: If you\xe2\x80\x99re one of these hundreds of billions of dollars GMV businesses, you want someone from the consulting companies who lives and breathes it.

Right, that makes sense. So another bit, you\xe2\x80\x99ve referenced this a couple of times, this is a meta point about being an analyst. You said before you love being in the markets. You have to write your stock picks thinking about what everyone else is doing. There\xe2\x80\x99s a bit here where you want to call Shopify before it inflects, right? I\xe2\x80\x99m just curious, I don\xe2\x80\x99t do stock picks, I\xe2\x80\x99ve always joked and/half very serious \xe2\x80\x94 if you want stock picks from me, I\xe2\x80\x99m going to charge MoffettNathanson prices, I\xe2\x80\x99m not going to be charging Stratechery prices. But I\xe2\x80\x99m just curious how you think about that as an analyst, because it\xe2\x80\x99s not just about looking at trends and figuring out what\xe2\x80\x99s going to happen. I think your bit about Shopify and enterprise makes total sense. I think your long-term bit about maybe in five, ten years it\xe2\x80\x99s all going to end up about Amazon anyway makes sense. But a big part of your job is just timing, how do you think about timing and knowing when to make a call or when not?

MM: I\xe2\x80\x99ll let you know when I have that answer perfect and boxed up on a bookshelf. I think all market participants are always learning and getting better. There\xe2\x80\x99s a couple ways I like to think about it in my seat, you enjoy the benefit of writing as the ultimate clarifier. If I have an idea, I think something\xe2\x80\x99s going to happen, if I can\xe2\x80\x99t write it in a way that can articulate the view, it most surely means I don\xe2\x80\x99t understand it as well as I\xe2\x80\x99d like to think. So the exercise of writing an investment thesis forces you to make it so it\xe2\x80\x99s digestible and also helps you build confidence that what you think is going to occur is likely to occur. That\xe2\x80\x99s point one. Now then the other side of that coin is trying to determine if everybody else knows that.

Right.

MM: That\xe2\x80\x99s the tricky part here. Now, if you\xe2\x80\x99re on the sell-side publishing research, a lot of our time is spent writing research and researching the information behind it. Then the other part of that is spent talking to people so you get a good idea what the market thinks about stuff.

We\xe2\x80\x99ll use Shopify for example, I\xe2\x80\x99ve talked to lots of Shopify investors, people who own the stock and don\xe2\x80\x99t own the stock, there was not this view, \xe2\x80\x9cHey, Shopify is going to succeed in the enterprise\xe2\x80\x9d. A lot of people weren\xe2\x80\x99t talking about it in the middle of 2023, I felt comfortable around that. Then beyond just our people talking about this topic, there are a lot of services that aggregate all the financial outlooks for all these companies. You see it, right? The news goes by, \xe2\x80\x9cXYZ company, beat Wall Street forecast by 2 cents.\xe2\x80\x9d That\xe2\x80\x99s the quantitative way to figure out if people also have the same outlook as you. So it\xe2\x80\x99s talking to market participants, but then seeing if it\xe2\x80\x99s reflected in the expectations for the given stock you\xe2\x80\x99re researching or making a call on at the time. Then you have to be comfortable being wrong in a very public forum on a frequent basis.

Yep. There is a bit where even if it\xe2\x80\x99s not fully clarified, you\xe2\x80\x99re putting a price on it, and prices are the most clarifying thing of all.

I have a couple other Shopify questions that just came off of notes of stuff that you\xe2\x80\x99ve written. You mentioned in passing in your note that there\xe2\x80\x99s no traction for Shopify Audiences. This is the Shopify Plus of offering where merchants can aggregate all their data to better buy ads on Facebook, it was a bit of a response to ATT, I was hugely in favor of this, I actually wrote they should do this . They announced it a week later, which to be clear it wasn\xe2\x80\x99t because I wrote it, we were thinking on the same wavelength. You said there\xe2\x80\x99s not really much take up for it, what\xe2\x80\x99s the hold up or why is it not resonating?

MM: So the thing with Audiences, I think we should pull back. In 2021 and 2022, as you\xe2\x80\x99ve talked about, ATT changes, it becomes harder to find your customers. At the same time, there\xe2\x80\x99s global logistics issues. Shopify set out to solve two of the main pain points for their merchants, fulfillment and traffic acquisition. Fulfillment is incredibly hard and scale matters, they divested it. Audiences is the other attempt to solve for one of the headaches. The way it works is merchants share data and you create lookalike audiences. Shopify has been rolling it out with some of the larger merchants, they have very promising ROAS. I think during the same time Meta got really good with working with the new construct in which they\xe2\x80\x99ve been forced to operate, same with Google, and they\xe2\x80\x99re just almost always going to win that, basically.

Basically, Meta and Google got better faster than Shopify Audiences to make a difference.

MM: Yes, they got better faster. I don\xe2\x80\x99t think merchants are really interested pulling their data if you\xe2\x80\x99re a sneaker company. Yeah.

Right. Even if they were close, \xe2\x80\x9cLook, oh, you\xe2\x80\x99re saying this option, I don\xe2\x80\x99t have to share my data to everybody else. I\xe2\x80\x99m going to go over there.\xe2\x80\x9d

MM: To be clear, Shopify disagrees with my perspective on this, but I also, I\xe2\x80\x99m not pulling this out of the air. When we did the upgrade, I spoke to a bunch of people who run Shopify Plus agencies and I was also just at an e-commerce conference. I ask everybody who will take the time to talk to me, \xe2\x80\x9cAre you interested in Audiences? Are you using it?\xe2\x80\x9d I never get a positive data point that, \xe2\x80\x9cHey, yes, we\xe2\x80\x99ve been using it, amazing product\xe2\x80\x9d. I would love to see it be successful. What I would love for Shopify if I was king is for the Shopify app to gain critical mass that aggregates shopping demand.

Right.

MM: That is how you try to solve for traffic acquisition. It\xe2\x80\x99s just really, really challenging.

Yeah, that\xe2\x80\x99s the dream, that\xe2\x80\x99s being a customer-facing aggregator. I don\xe2\x80\x99t know, I think that that\xe2\x80\x99s one of those things that makes theoretical sense, so that\xe2\x80\x99s what they should do. But to your bit, what makes Shopify so resonant for these big companies? Look, just remove the bottlenecks, we love your culture, we love the way you\xe2\x80\x99re building, the way you\xe2\x80\x99re delivering. The culture is not a \xe2\x80\x94 you mentioned B2C, but B2C was in the context of small individual merchants. Real B2C, like acquiring a billion users for your app, that\xe2\x80\x99s just not what Shopify does. So even though it makes theoretical sense, I\xe2\x80\x99ve always been skeptical of that becoming a huge thing.

MM: Yeah, that\xe2\x80\x99s why I said if I was king and I could control it or foresee the future\xe2\x80\x94

Right, if you could dictate it, you don\xe2\x80\x99t have to actually acquire all those users, yeah, that\xe2\x80\x99s a good point.

MM: \xe2\x80\x9cEverybody download Shop App and start using it for your shopping experience\xe2\x80\x9d, that would make an incredible amount of sense for the independent DTC merchants, it\xe2\x80\x99s hard to gain that type of traction.

Another question about Shopify. You\xe2\x80\x99ve mentioned the possibility of Shopify omnichannel and companies having their in-store retail purchases linked up with their online stores, is it any good? It feels like Adyen is going to run away with this. I guess what direction it\xe2\x80\x99s going to go? If you\xe2\x80\x99re Shopify, your channel starts online, and then for a Warby Parker, there\xe2\x80\x99s an offline store. I don\xe2\x80\x99t know what Warby Parker\xe2\x80\x99s solution is, I\xe2\x80\x99m just picking them out thin air, but it seems like an omnichannel \xe2\x80\x94 let me reframe the question. What direction does omnichannel run? Is it retailers with physical stores going online, they want a common solution, or is it online stores going offline because they want more predictable customer acquisition costs and that\xe2\x80\x99s the way it goes? Or am I thinking about this all wrong?

MM: I think you\xe2\x80\x99re thinking about it correctly, but I think it\xe2\x80\x99s going both ways. The DTC companies, Allbirds, Warby Parker, FIGS, are all opening physical retail stores. The DTC brands that started online are now moving to wholesale distribution channels within Walmart and Macy\xe2\x80\x99s of the world. It\xe2\x80\x99s all getting mashed together and merging, and Shopify has a point of sales solution to help with that. What\xe2\x80\x99s really interesting about Shopify\xe2\x80\x99s business, and I struggled to grasp it for a period of time, it\xe2\x80\x99s unique in how they ring the register. So we all know it as this software solution to provide an e-commerce platform, but 2/3 of the gross profits come from the Merchant Solutions segment. Within that is their payments business, basically how you\xe2\x80\x99re checking out and the capital business, they loan people money. They pull the money back, their shipping business. It\xe2\x80\x99s unique where imagine if Microsoft captured a percentage of the productivity increase from the knowledge-based economy when they rolled out Excel, right?

Right.

MM: They would be a $100 trillion company. Imagine if Adobe earned a percentage of every sale from a compelling creative advertisement that was made, Shopify, if they rolled out innovation in the next year that was so good at doubled GMV, they\xe2\x80\x99d participate in the upside. So I love the payments aspect that this company has and their online and offline solution, they really participate in the merchant success, which makes it unique.

Yeah, I think that\xe2\x80\x99s one of those things, though. It\xe2\x80\x99s like if we just get 1% of this market, imagine how much money we can make. We\xe2\x80\x99re out of time, I have a few other ones to hit.

Buy With Prime

Another bit that was interesting, just a passing line in this note, clients are not interested in Buy with Prime, maybe a surprise to people paying attention, people who love Prime. What\xe2\x80\x99s the hold up there?

MM: So we monitor Buy with Prime traffic. We have a solution, Similarweb, I\xe2\x80\x99m sure you\xe2\x80\x99ve been on the website, we subscribe to an extended version and you can actually see the checkouts when they\xe2\x80\x99re sent from a standalone website to the Buy with Prime website. It\xe2\x80\x99s growing traction but when you talk to merchants, there\xe2\x80\x99s an attempt, we talked about the headaches and concerns about putting your whole business on Amazon, and it\xe2\x80\x99s this last ditch effort of separation in church and state. I can have my Amazon business here, and if I have my standalone DTC business powered by Shopify and I use a third-party logistics company right now that\xe2\x80\x99s providing my fulfillment, I would like it to be separate. That\xe2\x80\x99s what\xe2\x80\x99s been shared with me when I talked to merchants or agency heads.

What is going to probably occur over time and what is already occurring today is that no one will be able to compete with Amazon\xe2\x80\x99s speed, scale, and cost. They\xe2\x80\x99re incredibly cost competitive for Fulfillment by Amazon, and they have multichannel fulfillment that shows up in unlabeled boxes and stuff. So as much as they don\xe2\x80\x99t like it now, this is going to be this thing. I think that you check the web traffic and just every month and year, it just ticks higher and higher.

Amazon\xe2\x80\x99s in it for the next 20 years, so maybe it\xe2\x80\x99s not a big deal now. All the reporting and scuttlebutt around this is Amazon was the one really pushing for this, Shopify was dragged into it a bit kicking and screaming, but is there still a real hang up as far as sharing data and things along those lines which make it hard? Because I think the dream from a merchant perspective is, \xe2\x80\x9cLook, we could just have all our stuff in one place in multiple storefronts\xe2\x80\x9d, but if Shopify is not sharing that data between the Shopify site and the warehouse, that gets tricky to manage. Is there any interest in solving that or is Shopify just sort of throwing up as many hurdles as possible while still nominally supporting it?

MM: I think the initial friction was because it\xe2\x80\x99s a very sensitive subject on handling the payments and who gets the payments revenue because that\xe2\x80\x99s the bread and butter for Shopify\xe2\x80\x99s business over the long term. It\xe2\x80\x99s where they ring the register. There is some aspect of the data sharing, I obviously have not seen the agreement that was reached by Shopify and Amazon, but from everything that I\xe2\x80\x99ve seen, the friction has been removed and now also it got a lot easier after they divested their logistics business because you had this payments business at risk and then you just bought this logistics business. It was just like headbutting and now you\xe2\x80\x99ve got terms on the payments.

And Amazon basically gave them payments. It was like, \xe2\x80\x9cYou can use your payments\xe2\x80\x9d, it really shows that they\xe2\x80\x99re at different goals here. To your point, Shopify wants to own the payments, wants to participate in the upside and Amazon is like, \xe2\x80\x9cLook, we\xe2\x80\x99re building the biggest logistics network the world has ever seen and we just want volume and we\xe2\x80\x99ll take it whatever way it gets and it\xe2\x80\x99ll pay off in 25 years\xe2\x80\x9d.

MM: And Amazon\xe2\x80\x99s fulfillment network is currently underutilized. During COVID, they doubled the square footage and at some point in 2020 and 2021, every single e-commerce company we cover overshot where they made an investment, they hired, they built infrastructure. That was when you clicked and dragged right in your model at one point and it just showed these mind-boggling GMV numbers that everybody had to build to overnight. So getting merchant adoption of Buy with Prime is simple math, you\xe2\x80\x99re pushing more volume through your network, ammortizing the costs over more units and it\xe2\x80\x99s a win-win for everybody. It\xe2\x80\x99s a win for the merchants on Shopify because they increase delivery speed. I\xe2\x80\x99m sure you\xe2\x80\x99ve seen it on the checkout versus the other checkouts. Your Buy with Prime tells you date of delivery, people love that, they know it\xe2\x80\x99s going to be here in a day-and-a-half and Amazon then moves more units through the fulfillment network. This is a one plus one equals three type of dynamic.

Amazon Advertising

What about Amazon advertising? It\xe2\x80\x99s a number three in the market, they\xe2\x80\x99ve kind of saturated Amazon.com as you\xe2\x80\x99ve written, how important is this them pushing off the site? They have this deal with Pinterest , they have a deal with Meta to link your accounts together. Is this just another, \xe2\x80\x9cWe need to push volume\xe2\x80\x9d sort of thing, or is this going to be a meaningful spanning the web sort of network?

MM: We did a tear down of Amazon\xe2\x80\x99s advertising business in 2023 where we gave our best attempt to model and forecast all the inputs. Twitch, Thursday Night Football, the DSP , Freevee, Fire TV, to try to figure out how big is the core advertising business at UNIC when we go on the website, sponsored products that are probably 50% of the icons that load up on your browser? It\xe2\x80\x99s 90% of the business. So as exciting as all the other stuff is, and you\xe2\x80\x99ll see a lot of hand waving around all the other stuff that they\xe2\x80\x99re doing, at the end of the day, this advertising business is going to be driven by unit sales because it\xe2\x80\x99s a seek-and-destroy platform.

So if units grow 10% on top of that, you\xe2\x80\x99re going to get some incremental ad load. I think Amazon still thinks there\xe2\x80\x99s room to go there and you\xe2\x80\x99ll get some pricing as advertisers view Amazon as not just an online channel. When you sit and listen to these brands and merchants talk, there is complete consensus that you can\xe2\x80\x99t look at them separately. You can\xe2\x80\x99t just look at them in some myopic ROAS calculation for this one unit we\xe2\x80\x99re selling. Online is impacting offline market share so there\xe2\x80\x99s room to run for Amazon\xe2\x80\x99s advertising product, but we always just like to clarify that 90% of it is the core retail media network.

This gets back to your worry for a company that commits to Amazon\xe2\x80\x99s long-term, which is, \xe2\x80\x9cOkay, fine. You\xe2\x80\x99re running away from the customer acquisition costs that are imposed on you by Meta and Google\xe2\x80\x9d, but what is happening is basically, \xe2\x80\x9cOh, you go to Amazon, you like all this traffic. Well, you\xe2\x80\x99re going to have to pay for that traffic over time\xe2\x80\x9d. That\xe2\x80\x99s just the pattern that happens and you see a lot of complaints about this from small, independent, totally commoditized products. But even if you have a good brand, if you\xe2\x80\x99re North Face jacket, how soon till Patagonia is buying North Face AdWords?

MM: It\xe2\x80\x99s a ruthlessly competitive market and you hear from a lot of frustrated merchants. This business, the growth of Amazon\xe2\x80\x99s advertising business is one of the most impressive things I think in all of consumer Internet in the last decade from effectively nothing to over a $40 billion run rate business, and there\xe2\x80\x99s a lot of merchants who were in the marketplace five or more years ago when it was simply less efficient. There was this first mover advantage where the ad buyers weren\xe2\x80\x99t all as sophisticated running as like off-site measurement tools and there was an arbitrage. Now, the floodgates have also opened to China-based merchants who are really sophisticated in the ad bidding and the margins have just compressed.

This is one of my biggest misses, I would say in my times of Stratechery, is not predicting this, because you talk about it being super impressive, but at the same time it\xe2\x80\x99s the exact thing we saw on Google, the exact same thing we saw on Facebook. If you have the audience and you have, particularly if that audience is search driven, it\xe2\x80\x99s just a matter of executing. Suddenly you\xe2\x80\x99re going to have to pay for what you used to get for free and all the complaints you hear from third-party merchants are all the complaints we heard previously from publishers, it\xe2\x80\x99s all the complaints previously we heard from game developers on Facebook, it\xe2\x80\x99s the same pattern playing over. Once you have the audience, you have the power.

MM: Amazon is getting better as well. When you talk to the company, \xe2\x80\x9cHey, how are you still putting up these numbers? This is mind-blowing\xe2\x80\x9d. Their responses are, \xe2\x80\x9cMeasurement tools that are improving. The auction is getting denser, so more merchants bidding\xe2\x80\x9d.

Makes a huge difference.

MM: They\xe2\x80\x99re not putting their feet up and just letting this thing run, they\xe2\x80\x99re improving day after day.

Walmart

We have a bunch of topics we didn\xe2\x80\x99t get to. There\xe2\x80\x99s one I did want to ask you about a company you don\xe2\x80\x99t cover is Walmart. Walmart, I think it\xe2\x80\x99s super interesting because what\xe2\x80\x99s the one area where \xe2\x80\x94 what\xe2\x80\x99s the weak point in Amazon? What\xe2\x80\x99s the thing they just struggle with and yet they\xe2\x80\x99re still throwing money at. It\xe2\x80\x99s grocery, right? It\xe2\x80\x99s like stuff that goes bad, it\xe2\x80\x99s perishable and it just fundamentally doesn\xe2\x80\x99t fit with their model and yet it\xe2\x80\x99s such a massive amount of retail that they feel compelled to keep going after it.

Meanwhile, Walmart struggled for years trying to duplicate Amazon and they went through multiple iterations of what their online product was. They finally over the last few years anchored on this where they\xe2\x80\x99re building up from pickup and delivery to actually incorporating their stores into a unified model, which is a massive lift to get to that case. Now, they\xe2\x80\x99re adding on the marketplace. There\xe2\x80\x99s this Vizio thing , they\xe2\x80\x99re just getting more display for their things. It seems pretty compelling from my perspective, is there any reason not to be super optimistic about what they\xe2\x80\x99re doing?

MM: Walmart and their moves into e-commerce are really exciting. Not a week goes by where I don\xe2\x80\x99t have a client interaction calling me saying, \xe2\x80\x9cI want to talk about what Walmart is doing\xe2\x80\x9d, or, \xe2\x80\x9cI just came from a meeting with Walmart. You should hear what they\xe2\x80\x99re saying about their intensions in e-commerce.\xe2\x80\x9d

The end state in US e-commerce is some mix of Amazon at Walmart running away with it. At the time, today, Walmart\xe2\x80\x99s business is a mini-Amazon, they are building out last mile fulfillment, they have their own gig delivery drivers with their Spark network, they have a retail media network, they have a DSP. They are building out fulfillment centers, they are taking the playbook and running with it and they have the best data set in the world outside of Amazon. They know what the customer who is on a GLP1 is now buying more fitness equipment and smoothies.

What is incredibly powerful about it, and I don\xe2\x80\x99t think we\xe2\x80\x99ll have time to get to this today, is their ability to really create a closed loop advertising system that can show incrementality to CPG companies.

It\xe2\x80\x99s so compelling. This is why the Vizio thing like this is amazing because they just have this burgeoning network and it\xe2\x80\x99s basically just in-store advertising right now. At what point are they giving away Vizio TVs for free? \xe2\x80\x9cLook, we need inventory, we need places to put ads\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94 it\xe2\x80\x99s like the pre-ATT world on steroids, \xe2\x80\x9cWe can track to the cent this ad resulted in this lift and that\xe2\x80\x99s really compelling\xe2\x80\x9d.

MM: It\xe2\x80\x99s a game changer. Whoever can really prove incrementality for these low volume growth industries like CPG wins an enormous advertising pool of dollars. The challenge is the CPG companies are sophisticated and they don\xe2\x80\x99t want to pay for transactions that were already going to happen. I always use the example, I consume an unhealthy amount of peanut butter every year and no peanut butter company wants to make themselves pay for Mike\xe2\x80\x99s peanut butter purchase because they know I was going to buy that brand anyways. Walmart is the one that has the ability to show that.

Now, your first question about what is something that Amazon keeps spending money on, grocery is the one problem Amazon has confronted where they\xe2\x80\x99re playing from behind. I think it\xe2\x80\x99s important to split, let\xe2\x80\x99s just call it the grocery store, what\xe2\x80\x99s sold under that roof into two umbrellas and it\xe2\x80\x99s perishables and what we refer to as non-discretionary. I think Walmart refers to in conversations with people as consumables. So in the perishables, that\xe2\x80\x99s groceries. That\xe2\x80\x99s like your eggs and beef and meat and then in the other side it\xe2\x80\x99s baby wipes and shampoo. And on the perishable aspect, Walmart has 4,000 distribution locations and they\xe2\x80\x99re the stores that cover 90% of America.

And the stores make sure it\xe2\x80\x99s fresh, right? So you can actually deliver it because you have a way to get rid of your non-fresh stuff.

MM: It\xe2\x80\x99s fresh and no one can rival the purchasing power of Walmart. So we\xe2\x80\x99ve done price comparisons in America\xe2\x80\x99s largest suburban markets for food delivery and Walmart is roughly 25% cheaper than the kind of average \xe2\x80\x94 Amazon is middle of the pack, I think Target is second place \xe2\x80\x94 and when you look at the non-discretionary stuff, the shampoos of the world, this is intuitive and I think makes sense that Walmart and Amazon are at parity, so that market is going to be split up.

I think Walmart just their focus is to win grocery due to the purchasing frequency and then they\xe2\x80\x99re going to try to convert people and make their money back on the general merchandise category. Amazon realizes they can\xe2\x80\x99t muscle their way into this. The legacy grocery industry has like a hundred year head start of them in stores in every town.

Well, it\xe2\x80\x99s totally counter to what Amazon does. Amazon is the most scale-focused company there is and plan everything they do around that. It\xe2\x80\x99s predicated on goods being non-perishable, they just have to be, it doesn\xe2\x80\x99t work otherwise.

MM: It\xe2\x80\x99s funny if you listen to Amazon\xe2\x80\x99s recent calls, it\xe2\x80\x99s what all the Q&A is, everyone is asking about the grocery business, everything else is running smoothly. AWS growth has picked back up, the retail margins are improving, but every investor sits there and thinks, \xe2\x80\x9cOh no, there\xe2\x80\x99s this one category that has\xe2\x80\xa6\xe2\x80\x9d, we estimate it\xe2\x80\x99s a $2 trillion addressable market. It\xe2\x80\x99s implied to be a big portion of the growth in e-commerce going forward. Amazon could sink a lot of money in this if they really wanted to, that\xe2\x80\x99s why everybody talks about it and it\xe2\x80\x99s so scary.

But I\xe2\x80\x99m not sure if it\xe2\x80\x99s going to work, which is why Walmart, to the extent Walmart, they can dominate that, then they can move into your point with a price advantage or distribution advantage or more importantly, a customer acquisition advantage in the stuff they do compete with Amazon with. I think it\xe2\x80\x99s really interesting, and I\xe2\x80\x99m pretty impressed with what Walmart has done, to be totally honest.

MM: Amazon is pretty frank about it on the calls. They\xe2\x80\x99ve tested a bunch of different models, they\xe2\x80\x99re trying to find one that works because they\xe2\x80\x99ve built these fresh fulfillment centers outside of cities where you have a warehouse full of groceries. You sort them into a bag and then try to get them to people, that doesn\xe2\x80\x99t really work well, and then you don\xe2\x80\x99t have enough Whole Foods to really compete door to door with Walmart. They\xe2\x80\x99re pretty open, they don\xe2\x80\x99t have the solution today for this problem.

I think the idea behind whole Whole Foods was very strong , it\xe2\x80\x99s still one of my most read articles ever explaining this bit about how perishables are different than non-perishables. Amazon needs that first customer for perishables so they can even build delivery around it. I think the problem is that they\xe2\x80\x99re not \xe2\x80\x94 to your point, there\xe2\x80\x99s not enough Whole Foods and they kind of shot their shot, they actually needed way more penetration to pull this off and now they\xe2\x80\x99ll never be allowed to buy another grocery chain.

MM: They can\xe2\x80\x99t even buy a robot vacuum cleaning company .

Well, this was really interesting. Like I said, we didn\xe2\x80\x99t get to Etsy or Shein or Temu, we had a bunch of stuff to get to, so we\xe2\x80\x99ll have to talk another time. But, Mike, it was great to have you on and super interesting what\xe2\x80\x99s happening and I think that this was very clarifying for me and I think it was for our listeners, so thanks for coming on.

MM: Ben, as a long time reader and listener, this has been an absolute pleasure. Thank you so much.
